Name: LatencyBoundedGeometricRouting
Title: Latency-Bounded Geometric Routing for Dynamic Inference Optimization
Short Hypothesis: Can we develop a training-free, latency-bounded geometric routing framework that dynamically optimizes inference paths by leveraging hidden-state geometry, ensuring strict wall-clock latency targets while maintaining or improving accuracy?
Related Work: Recent works such as 'Spectral Quantization Routing' and 'Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation' have explored the use of activation geometry for precision allocation and noise cancellation. However, these methods often focus on specific aspects like quantization or noise cancellation and do not provide a comprehensive framework for dynamic inference optimization under strict latency constraints. This proposal aims to bridge this gap by integrating spectral geometry with latency-aware routing, offering a unified approach to optimize inference paths in real-time.
Abstract: Dynamic inference optimization is crucial for real-world deployment of large language models (LLMs) and other deep learning systems. Existing methods often rely on static profiling or costly calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions while maintaining strict latency budgets. We propose Latency-Bounded Geometric Routing (LBGR), a training-free framework that dynamically optimizes inference paths by leveraging hidden-state geometry. LBGR computes real-time activation covariances and spectral diagnostics to identify robust and sensitive subspaces, guiding tokens through optimized layers or attention heads. This ensures strict wall-clock latency targets while maintaining or improving accuracy. We theoretically ground LBGR in PAC-Bayes bounds and spectral stability metrics, demonstrating its effectiveness across diverse benchmarks and under natural distribution shifts. LBGR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference, bridging the gap between foundational optimization theory and practical, scalable deployment.
Experiments: Compute real-time activation covariances and spectral diagnostics using randomized SVD on a subset of hidden states.
Implement a dynamic routing mechanism that maps tokens to optimized layers or attention heads based on the computed spectral diagnostics.
Evaluate the method on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, including WILDS, GLUE, and adversarial ICL benchmarks, measuring throughput, accuracy, and wall-clock latency.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of spectral diagnostics on inference optimization and robustness to distributional shifts.
Compare LBGR against static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines, quantifying the performance gains in terms of throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of real-time spectral diagnostics may introduce additional latency, which needs to be carefully managed to ensure strict wall-clock targets.
The effectiveness of the spectral diagnostics in identifying robust and sensitive subspaces may vary across different models and datasets, requiring thorough validation.

The dynamic routing mechanism may introduce new optimization pathologies, such as overfitting to specific input distributions, which need to be addressed through robustness evaluation and regularization techniques.
